title: Children's Privacy under DPbD
description: Special protections for processing personal data of children, as proposed in the JPDP DPbD Guideline (issued 30 April 2026; PCP No. 2/2025 substantive content). Children merit specific protection due to lower awareness of risks, consequences, safeguards, and rights.

scope.appliesTo: Providers of products or services directed at, intended for, or likely to be accessed by children — including offline (educational, sports, social clubs, health/social support) and digital (websites, apps, IoT, social media, gaming, entertainment, advocacy).
scope.scopeNote: Covers services that a significant number of children use in reality, even if not primarily intended for children.

bestInterestsOfChild.principle: Data controllers must take into account the best interests of children as a primary consideration where any conflict arises. Source: Article 3 of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child.
considerations: Keep children safe from exploitation (commercial, sexual, abuse)
Protect and support health and wellbeing
Protect and support physical, psychological, emotional development
Protect children's need to develop their own views and identity
Protect freedom of association and play
Support the needs of children with disabilities
Recognise the role of parents in protecting and promoting children's best interests
Recognise the evolving capacity of the child to form their own view

ageVerification.obligation: Data controllers must make reasonable efforts to verify they have received consent to process children's personal data; in certain circumstances this entails establishing age verification mechanisms. Approach should be proportionate and risk-based — greater stringency where processing carries higher risk.

mechanisms.note: Most common but easily bypassed; suitable only for low-risk processing or as one of several techniques.
Used by e-commerce sites for adult products. Cannot ascertain card user is owner; minimum credit card age varies by country.
AI-powered. Accuracy issues, privacy concerns due to sensitive data use, risk of profiling.
Browsing history, maturity questionnaires, online content/purchase analysis.
Scratch cards or in-situ ID checks at retail outlets.
Traditional ID document checks; for higher-risk: proof of ID; for lower-risk: confirmation email.
Third party (not parent) confirms child's age.
State-issued tools (China, Canada, Australia, EU digital identity wallet).

ageVerification.antiLockoutPrinciple: Compliance does not justify locking children out of services or providing inferior two-tier offerings. Doing so risks driving children to lie about their age and access services without child-appropriate protections.

dpbdElementsForChildren.language: Clear, concise, child-friendly language explaining what is done with their personal data.
dpbdElementsForChildren.accessibility: Information in obvious, easy-to-find places. Not in tiny writing. Not designed to nudge acceptance. Consider device used (phone, computer, IoT, toys), use of cartoons/videos, layered/hover/pop-up notices.
dpbdElementsForChildren.context: Not only at sign-up. Just-in-time notifications when data is shared (e.g., before posting, before changing default privacy settings).
dpbdElementsForChildren.parentalDashboard: Where appropriate, provide parents with overall view of activity and settings.
dpbdElementsForChildren.rightsExercise: Dedicated, clear, child-friendly user flows. For automated tools handling DSARs, design for circumstances requiring individual (non-automated) assessment for child subjects.

prohibitionOnProfiling.rule: Data controllers must not profile children, engage in automated decision-making concerning children, or use their personal data for advertising/marketing purposes — unless they can clearly demonstrate how and why it is in the best interests of children to do so.
prohibitionOnProfiling.crossReference: automated-decisions/index.json — ADMP module covers the general rule on automated decision-making and profiling; this section adds the children-specific prohibition.
